items.question_text: What is the basic operating concept for an en route or oceanic sector team?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility, and the team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the sector.
Only the radar position is responsible for sector operation once aircraft are on frequency.
Only the radar coordinator is responsible for sector objectives when that position is staffed.
Responsibilities change completely depending on whether one, two, or three people are working the sector.

items.explanation: The tasks remain the same regardless of staffing count, and the sector team as a whole owns safe and efficient operation.

items.question_text: The term Radar Position refers to the en route team position in direct communication with aircraft that uses radar information as the primary means of separation.
items.explanation: That statement matches the standardized definition of the en route Radar Position.

items.question_text: When the Radar Coordinator Position is staffed, controllers must keep aircraft-separation responsibility with the Radar Position while the coordinator manages traffic flows.
items.explanation: Staffing the Radar Coordinator Position shifts traffic-flow management, not aircraft-separation responsibility.

items.question_text: If the Radar Associate accepts or initiates automated handoff actions necessary for smooth sector operation, the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of the action taken.
items.explanation: The Radar Associate may help with automated handoff actions, but the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of them.

items.situation: An en route sector is busy. The Radar Associate accepts an automated handoff to keep traffic moving, updates the strip, and plans to tell the Radar Position after finishing another coordination call. At the same time, the Radar Coordinator says the coordinator now owns separation because the coordinator is handling traffic flow.
items.para_context: In an en route sector, the Radar Associate may accept or initiate necessary automated handoffs but must ensure the Radar Position is made immediately aware of the action. When the Radar Coordinator Position is staffed, it assumes responsibility for managing traffic flows, while the Radar Position retains responsibility for aircraft separation.

choices.text: Make the Radar Position immediately aware of the automated handoff action, and keep aircraft-separation responsibility with the Radar Position.
Let the Radar Associate finish all coordination before informing the Radar Position, and transfer separation responsibility to the Radar Coordinator.
Cancel the automated handoff because associates may never take automated handoff actions.
Treat the Radar Coordinator as the radio-and-separation position any time the coordinator is staffed.

items.explanation: Automated handoff help from the Radar Associate does not remove the need to keep the Radar Position immediately aware, and coordinator staffing does not transfer aircraft separation.

items.question_text: What is the basic operating concept for a terminal radar or nonradar team?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility, and the team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector.
The radar position alone owns facility operation once aircraft are on frequency.
The radar associate owns coordination and separation whenever more than one controller is working.
Responsibilities are reduced when one person works the position because some tasks no longer apply.

items.explanation: The tasks remain the same regardless of staffing, and the team as a whole is responsible for safe and efficient operation.

items.question_text: The term Radar Associate Position refers to the terminal position commonly called Handoff Controller or Radar Data Controller.
items.explanation: That is the standardized common name for the terminal Radar Associate Position.

items.question_text: The terminal Nonradar Position uses nonradar procedures as the primary means of separation while usually communicating directly with aircraft.
items.explanation: That is the standardized definition of the terminal Nonradar Position.

items.question_text: If the terminal Radar Associate accepts or initiates an automated handoff needed for smooth sector operation, the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of the action taken.
items.explanation: The Radar Associate may help with automated handoffs, but the Radar Position must be made immediately aware.

items.situation: In a terminal radar sector, the Radar Associate accepts an automated handoff to keep traffic flowing, makes the computer entry, and assumes the Radar Position can discover the change later by looking at the scope. Another controller argues that because the associate handled the action, the associate now owns separation for that aircraft until further notice.
items.para_context: The terminal Radar Associate may accept or initiate automated handoffs necessary for continued smooth operation, but the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of any actions taken. The Radar Position remains the direct control and primary radar separation position.

choices.text: Make the Radar Position immediately aware of the action and keep separation responsibility with the Radar Position.
Let the automation speak for itself and treat the Radar Associate as the temporary separation position.
Prohibit the Radar Associate from ever touching automated handoffs, even when necessary for smooth operation.
Delay telling the Radar Position until the aircraft is transferred to the next facility.

items.explanation: The associate may help the sector keep moving, but the Radar Position must know immediately and remains the primary separation position.

items.question_text: What is the basic operating concept for a tower team?

choices.text: There are no absolute divisions of responsibility, and the tower team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector.
The local controller alone owns all tower tasks whenever traffic is moving.
The coordinator position takes over all duties whenever it is staffed.
Only the flight data or clearance position is responsible for tower-cab objectives when traffic is light.

items.explanation: The tower team concept mirrors the other team-position sections: the tasks remain the same and the team as a whole owns the operation.

items.question_text: The term Tower Position refers to LC or GC, the positions in direct communication with aircraft that ensure separation in or on the area of jurisdiction.
items.explanation: That is the standardized definition of the tower positions LC or GC.

items.question_text: Aircraft-separation responsibility remains with the Tower Positions when the Tower Cab Coordinator Position is staffed.
items.explanation: Coordinator staffing changes traffic-flow management, not who keeps aircraft-separation responsibility.

items.question_text: When a Tower Associate accepts or initiates coordination for smooth cab operation, that controller must ensure the Tower Position is made immediately aware of the action taken.
items.explanation: Tower associates may help with coordination, but the Tower Position must be made immediately aware of actions taken.

items.situation: A tower associate accepts coordination for a runway crossing to keep the cab moving and plans to tell the local controller later because everyone can see the strips. At the same time, the tower coordinator says that because the coordinator is staffed, the local controller no longer owns aircraft separation for the runway operation.
items.para_context: Tower Associate Positions may accept or initiate coordination for the continued smooth operation of the tower cab, but they must ensure the Tower Position is made immediately aware of actions taken. When the Tower Cab Coordinator Position is staffed, it manages traffic flows while the Tower Positions retain aircraft-separation responsibility.

choices.text: Ensure the Tower Position is made immediately aware of the coordination action, and keep aircraft-separation responsibility with the Tower Position.
Let the Tower Associate finish the coordination silently and transfer separation responsibility to the coordinator.
Treat the Tower Associate as the separation position any time it accepts or initiates coordination.
Stop using tower associates for coordination because only the coordinator may assist the tower positions.

items.explanation: Tower associates can help the cab keep moving, but the tower position must know immediately, and coordinator staffing does not move aircraft separation away from the tower position.

items.question_text: Which en route team position is sometimes referred to as D-Side or Manual Controller?
items.explanation: The Radar Associate is the en route team position sometimes called D-Side or Manual Controller.

items.question_text: Which en route team position is commonly referred to as Assistant Controller or A-Side?
items.explanation: Radar Flight Data is the en route team position commonly referred to as Assistant Controller or A-Side.

items.question_text: Which en route team position usually communicates directly with aircraft and uses nonradar procedures as the primary means of separation?
items.explanation: That definition belongs to the Nonradar Position.

items.question_text: What must the en route Radar Associate do after accepting or initiating automated handoff actions necessary for the smooth operation of the sector?
items.explanation: The Radar Associate may help with those handoff actions, but the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of any action taken.

choices.text: Ensure the Radar Position is made immediately aware of any action taken.
Assume aircraft-separation responsibility until the handoff is complete.
Wait to advise the Radar Position until after the next point out is complete.
Convert every automated handoff into a nonautomated handoff.

items.question_text: When the Radar Coordinator Position is staffed, the Radar Position retains responsibility for aircraft separation.
items.explanation: The coordinator assumes responsibility for managing traffic flows, while the Radar Position retains aircraft separation.

items.question_text: Which en route team position uses radar information as the primary means of separation and communicates directly with aircraft?
items.explanation: That definition belongs to the Radar Position.

items.question_text: What is the common name for the terminal Radar Associate Position?
items.explanation: The terminal Radar Associate Position is commonly referred to as Handoff Controller or Radar Data Controller.

choices.text: Handoff Controller or Radar Data Controller.

items.question_text: Which set of common names belongs to the terminal Radar Coordinator Position?
items.explanation: The terminal Radar Coordinator Position is commonly referred to as Coordinator, Tracker, Sequencer, or Overhead.

choices.text: Handoff Controller, D-Side, or Manual Controller.
Local Assist, Ground Assist, or Clearance.

items.question_text: Which terminal radar team position is commonly referred to simply as Flight Data?
items.explanation: Radar Flight Data is the terminal radar team position commonly referred to as Flight Data.

items.question_text: What must happen after the terminal Radar Associate accepts or initiates an automated handoff needed for smooth operation?
items.explanation: The Radar Associate may take the action, but the Radar Position must be made immediately aware of it.

choices.text: The Radar Position must be made immediately aware of the action taken.
The Radar Coordinator must assume separation responsibility for the aircraft.
The automated handoff must be replaced with a manual one.
The action may remain unannounced until the aircraft is transferred.

items.question_text: Which terminal radar team position usually communicates directly with aircraft and uses nonradar procedures as the primary means of separation?
items.explanation: That definition belongs to the terminal Nonradar Position.

items.question_text: The terminal radar team as a whole is responsible for the safe and efficient operation of the facility or sector, regardless of whether one, two, or three people are working.
items.explanation: The team concept applies regardless of whether one, two, or three people are working positions.

items.question_text: Which tower team position is commonly referred to as Clearance?
items.explanation: Clearance Delivery is the tower team position commonly referred to as Clearance.

items.question_text: Which tower team position is commonly referred to as Local Assist, Ground Assist, Local Associate, or Ground Associate?
items.explanation: Those are the standardized common names for the Tower Associate Position.

items.question_text: Which tower team position issues clearances and ensures the accuracy of pilot read back?
items.explanation: Those are core Clearance Delivery responsibilities.

items.question_text: Which tower team position observes and reports weather information?
items.explanation: Observing and reporting weather information is listed under Flight Data responsibilities.

items.question_text: After the Tower Associate accepts or initiates coordination for smooth cab operation, what must also occur?
items.explanation: The Tower Associate can help with coordination, but the Tower Position must be made immediately aware of the actions taken.

choices.text: The Tower Position must be made immediately aware of the action taken.
The Tower Associate temporarily assumes aircraft-separation responsibility.
The Tower Coordinator must approve each action before the Tower Position is told.
The action may remain unannounced if it is reflected on the strip bay.

items.question_text: When the Tower Cab Coordinator Position is staffed, the Tower Positions retain responsibility for aircraft separation.
items.explanation: The coordinator manages traffic flows when staffed, but the Tower Positions retain aircraft-separation responsibility.
